title: Doratoxylon apetalum
intro: Doratoxylon apetalum, also known as the ironwood tree, is a species of plant native to the tropical regions of Southeast Asia. It is valued for its medicinal properties, which include its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic activities, making it beneficial for treating fever, wounds, and other health issues. The bioactive constituents of D. apetalum include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, D. apetalum is commonly used in various herbal preparations, including infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, to treat a range of ailments, particularly fever and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Doratoxylon apetalum is classified under the family Sapindaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the soapberry family. This plant belongs to the order Sapindales, a group of plants characterized by their simple and alternate leaves. The genus Doratoxylon is a monotypic genus, meaning it contains only one species, which is D. apetalum. The classification of D. apetalum further includes its family placement under the subfamily Dodonaeoideae. Overall, the comprehensive classification of D. apetalum provides insight into its evolutionary relationships with other plant species.
uses: Doratoxylon apetalum is a plant species native to the tropical forests of Sri Lanka, and its bark and leaves have been traditionally used in folk medicine. The plant's medicinal properties are attributed to its ability to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. Traditionally, the bark of Doratoxylon apetalum is used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The plant's leaves are also used to treat digestive issues and as an antiseptic to reduce inflammation. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal potential and its effects on human health.
